entry.message: Sandbox evaluated community message from sandbox.user.

input.message:
I was removed from this project or not? can you please help me to check?

6a04fc6198241a93dfaeb218

decision.reason: User is asking about account status and eligibility, which are sensitive account policy matters that require verification by a human agent. This is not covered by project memory or guidelines.
guidelineSnippets:
If itʼs not bug injection, please upload an empty basetoinstance.patch file to the task
Please note that the Outlier platform does not allow literally empty files. Adding an empty
line or a space is acceptable)
2.4 Test the Issue Message (return to TOC
Make sure the issue message is challenging enough to cause at least one model to fail.
To do so, open only the App/ folder in Cursor and have both models available: Sonnet 4.6
and Qwen 3.5 27b, if you donʼt have them available go to the Environment Setup section
in the appendix to see a full guide on how to set them up.
● If it is a Bug injection task, make sure the app/ folder contains the bug
● Paste the issue message and select one model (Sonnet 4.6 or Qwen 3.5 27b). Then
check whether it fails to create a valid solution.
● Repeat the same with the other model.
● If both models solve the issue correctly, rewrite or adjust the issue message to
increase its complexity or change the issue topic until one of them fails.
● Ensure that the reported failure is directly tied to a specific requirement described in
the issue message. The model should only fail when it does not correctly implement
an expected behavior explicitly requested in the prompt, not because of
assumptions, inferred expectations, or invented edge cases that are not part of the
original issue description. If you want to know about valid failures refer to the
Sample Invalid Model Failures section.
● When at least one model fails, and the issue message meets all requirements, select
"Yes" and continue with the task.
● Extract the model failure trace from Cursor as documentation. If both models fail,
capturing one of them is enough). If you need a guide on how to generate that trace,
go to the How to generate the model trace section.
● Generate a model_trace.patch containing the exact changes made by the model
during its trace. If you need a guide on how to generate that file, go to the How to
generate model_trace.patch section.
IMPORTANT Ensure that only the App/ folder is opened in Cursor so the
models do not have access to the patches with the solution (you can open a
new Cursor window with just the App/ content).

4. Run tests on fixed state Phase 2 It should run the tests again on the fixed
code and show the full output.
5. Run cleanly: The script should complete successfully using the expected flow,
without patch application issues or script-level failures.
6. For Migration tasks that require installing new dependencies or modifications to
the package.json, please check Installing dependencies in test_patch.patch
section in the Appendix for a how-to-do-it guideline.
IMPORTANT: All the repository dependencies and the necessary assets
(env variables, etc) should be installed/configured in the
base.Dockerfile.
This also includes extra test-only dependencies that are needed to run
the tests, even if they are not included in the original repository and are
not added by the gold patch. For example, if the tests need playwright,
it and anything else required for it to run must be set up in the
base.Dockerfile.
The run_script.sh should be focused ONLY on applying the patches
and running the tests for each phase.
It must not install ANY dependencies or run npm install (or similar).
4.2 Create Parse Script (return to TOC
● Location: scripts/parse_results.sh
● Template: Use the parse_results.sh template in the Templates document and match
the run_script.sh output.
● The script should:
1. Parse test output from the run_script.sh
2. Identify Phase 1 results (broken state)
3. Identify Phase 2 results (fixed state)
4. Determine the overall result. Should output “SUCCESSˮ if all F2P failed and all
P2P passed in phase 1, and all F2P/P2P passed in phase 2.
5. Output results to a JSON file test_results.json with the required output
format
Required Output Format

Changelog
Date Updates
June 10 → NEW Step 0 - Project Onboarding
May 28 → model_trace.patch is now required for model failure trace
Project Overview
The purpose of this project is to create a database of challenging issue requests for a
repository. This database will be used to benchmark and improve the ability of coding
agents to implement changes in a codebase in response to user prompts.
To achieve this, you will build a standard project setup around an assigned repository. You
will then create a challenging prompt requesting changes to the codebase. You will check
that the model fails to respond adequately to your prompt, write your own solution to the
issue, create unit tests to verify your solution, and compose a rubric that can be used to
grade how successful the agent was in responding to your issue. When you are finished,
you will prepare the required patches, scripts, and Docker files so the whole workflow can
be reproduced and validated later.
